Rename the GitHub repository aegean-ai/aiops-control-plane to aegean-ai/auraison-control-plane.

Steps:
1. gh api repos/aegean-ai/aiops-control-plane --method PATCH --field name=auraison-control-plane
2. Update git remote: git remote set-url origin https://github.com/aegean-ai/auraison-control-plane.git
3. Update all references to the old name in: CLAUDE.md, README.md, docs/plans/*.md, pyproject.toml
4. Update beads prefix: bd rename-prefix auraison-control-plane
5. Verify: git push and gh repo view to confirm
title: Rename repo from aiops-control-plane to auraison-control-plane

description:
## Context

The current architecture conflates two distinct agent roles under a single mechanism (claude -p subprocesses):

1. **Claude Code as platform developer** — the agent that writes, tests, and maintains the Auraison platform itself. This is its primary and natural scope: software development, infra management, beads issue tracking, documentation.
2. **Claude Code as runtime operator** — NotebookAgent, ClusterAgent, WandBAgent, LakehouseAgent, TwinAgent, CosmosAgent are all claude -p subprocesses that power the runtime functions of the control, data, and user planes.

This conflation is an acknowledged v1 pragmatic choice (CLAUDE.md: 'Pydantic AI integration deferred to v2'). It works at small scale but creates structural problems as the platform grows:
- Runtime agents inherit the full surface area and cost of Claude Code subscriptions
- Tool scoping is enforced only by prompt strings, not by framework contracts
- Composability is limited: agents cannot be wired together programmatically without subprocess chains
- Testing runtime agent logic requires invoking claude -p, not unit tests
- The plane separation (user / control / data) is not reflected in agent architecture

## Proposed direction

**Claude Code** remains the agent for platform development: writing code, running tests, managing beads issues, authoring design docs, infra operations via kubectl/helm. It is the developer of the platform, not a runtime component of it.

**Pydantic AI agents** power all plane runtime functions:
- Control plane: job dispatch, cluster health, experiment tracking, twin sync
- Data plane: lakehouse catalog, ingestion pipelines, query routing
- User plane: VLA reasoning, behavior tree execution (Cosmos-Reason2 as model backbone)
- Management plane (v2): billing events, quota enforcement

Pydantic AI provides typed tool definitions, composable agent graphs, structured output, and testable agent logic — exactly what runtime plane agents need. Cosmos-Reason2 (auraison-eh1) and future open-weight models serve as the model backend for user-plane and data-plane agents; Claude (via API or Code) remains the backend for platform-level reasoning.

## Scope of this epic

1. **ADR**: Document the decision in docs/decisions/ — Claude Code = platform dev, Pydantic AI = runtime agents
2. **Design doc**: docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-pydantic-ai-agent-architecture.md — per-plane agent graph, tool registry, model routing (Claude API for control plane, Cosmos-Reason2 for user plane)
3. **Control plane migration**: Replace NotebookAgent, ClusterAgent, WandBAgent, LakehouseAgent subprocess wrappers with Pydantic AI agents; keep run_agent() as a shim during transition
4. **Data plane migration**: LakehouseCatalog agent, ingestion agent, query agent as Pydantic AI
5. **User plane**: VLA agent and behavior tree executor as Pydantic AI with Cosmos-Reason2 backend
6. **TwinAgent**: Implement from the start as Pydantic AI (auraison-5z3)
7. **Testing**: Unit-testable agent logic without invoking claude -p
8. **FastAPI wiring**: Replace subprocess invocations with Pydantic AI agent.run() calls

## Requirements before sub-issues

Per CLAUDE.md planning policy, an ADR (docs/decisions/) and a design doc (docs/plans/) are required before sub-issues are created. The design doc must cover:
- Agent graph topology per plane
- Tool registry design (how tools are scoped per agent type)
- Model routing strategy (which model backs which agent)
- Migration path: claude -p shim → Pydantic AI without breaking v1 behaviour
- Testing strategy for Pydantic AI agents
title: Decouple Claude Code (platform dev) from Pydantic AI (plane runtime agents)
Frontend — build out jobs, clusters, experiments UI pages

description:
Migrate the Quarto book at /home/pantelis.monogioudis/local/ai/apps/computer-vision/auraison-app/docs.auraison.app into the auraison monorepo under docs/.

## Source

- Path: /home/pantelis.monogioudis/local/ai/apps/computer-vision/auraison-app/docs.auraison.app
- Format: Quarto book (_quarto.yml, 37 .qmd files)
- Top-level sections: PRD/, ADD/, SDD/ (each with evidence-agent/ subdirectory), index.qmd, README.qmd, superceded/

## Target

- Destination: docs/ in the auraison monorepo (alongside existing architecture/, decisions/, plans/)
- Format: plain Markdown (.md) — no Quarto-specific syntax

## What to do

1. For each non-superceded .qmd file (6 active files: PRD/evidence-agent/, ADD/evidence-agent/, SDD/evidence-agent/, index.qmd, README.qmd):
   - Strip Quarto YAML front-matter (keep title, replace with standard --- title/description front-matter)
   - Convert Quarto callouts (:::{.callout-note} etc.) to plain Markdown equivalents (> **Note:** ...)
   - Convert Quarto cross-refs (#sec-, @fig-, @tbl-) to plain anchor links
   - Rename .qmd → .md
   - Place in matching path under docs/ (e.g., docs/PRD/evidence-agent/index.md)

2. For superceded/ content (31 .qmd files): migrate as-is to docs/superceded/ with .qmd → .md rename and minimal front-matter cleanup. Do not do deep content rewrites.

3. Do NOT migrate _quarto.yml, _book/, _freeze/, _site/, Makefile, buildspec.yml, docker/, or build artifacts.

4. Update docs/README.md (or create one) to reflect the new structure.

5. Commit under docs/: "docs: migrate docs.auraison.app Quarto site; convert .qmd → .md"

## Acceptance criteria

- All 37 .qmd files have a corresponding .md in docs/
- No .qmd syntax remains in active (non-superceded) files
- Existing docs/ structure (architecture/, decisions/, plans/) is untouched
- `find docs/ -name "*.qmd"` returns empty
title: Migrate docs.auraison.app Quarto site into auraison/docs/

description:
## Context

/home/pantelis.monogioudis/local/devops/apps/architecture/building-generative-ai-services
is the companion repo for the O'Reilly book *Building Generative AI Services with FastAPI*
(behind paywall — local access available). It covers production patterns for FastAPI +
GenAI services with strong event-based and async-first design.

## Goal

Produce docs/decisions/NNN-fastapi-genai-patterns.md (ADR format) that maps the book's
patterns to auraison's open architecture gaps. The ADR should give concrete guidance for
the issues currently in flight.

## Relevant patterns from the book (by auraison concern)

### 1. Async job dispatch — auraison-dji.2 (Redis queue)
- Ch05: asyncio.gather() / asyncio.wait() for parallel agent invocations
- Ch10: slowapi + Redis for rate limiting (already planned)
- BackgroundTasks pattern for fire-and-forget job submission
- Contrast with auraison's current subprocess-based agent model

### 2. Live job status — auraison-3hy.4 (SSE / WebSocket)
- Ch06: SSE via StreamingResponse + async generators (simplest path)
- Ch06: WSConnectionManager class pattern for WebSocket fan-out
- Recommendation: SSE for job polling (stateless), WebSocket only if push to N clients needed

### 3. Agent streaming output — control-plane agents
- Ch05/Ch06: async generators + itertools.tee() for multi-consumer streams
- Ch06: Azure OpenAI async streaming → chunked SSE
- Maps to: claude -p subprocess stdout → SSE to frontend

### 4. Middleware observability — missing from auraison
- main.py: request ID (UUID), perf_counter timing, CSV request log, status code metrics
- Ch02: Loguru structured logging
- OpenTelemetry instrumentation hooks
- Recommendation: add FastAPI middleware for request tracing before dji wiring lands

### 5. Content guardrails / AgentOps runtime stabilisation
- Ch09: concurrent guardrail evaluation (topical + moderation) via asyncio.wait()
- Score-based rejection with graceful error responses
- Maps to: AgentOps v1.5 — agent output validation before returning to control plane

### 6. SQLAlchemy async + Alembic — auraison-dji.1 (Postgres job store)
- Ch07: SQLAlchemy 2.0 async ORM, entity relationships (Conversations → Messages)
- Alembic migration pattern (separate migrations/ directory)
- Recommendation: adopt same pattern for Jobs → Events table

### 7. Auth layering — management plane (v2, but ADR now)
- Ch08: HTTP Basic → OAuth2 (GitHub) → JWT progression
- RBAC pattern and session-based token deactivation
- Captures design intent before management plane implementation starts

## Deliverable

A single ADR at docs/decisions/ with:
- Decision record header (date, status: proposed, deciders)
- One section per concern above
- Concrete recommendation (adopt / adapt / defer) with rationale
- Code sketch or file pointer for each adopted pattern
- Cross-references to open beads issues

## Source material

All patterns can be verified directly from:
  /home/pantelis.monogioudis/local/devops/apps/architecture/building-generative-ai-services/
    app/main.py           — middleware, lifespan, endpoints
    app/schemas.py        — Pydantic patterns
    examples/ch05/        — async patterns
    examples/ch06/        — SSE, WebSocket
    examples/ch07/        — SQLAlchemy + Alembic
    examples/ch09/        — guardrails
    examples/ch10/        — caching, rate limiting
title: arch: review building-generative-ai-services patterns → auraison ADR

description:
Epic covering the design and implementation of digital-twin construction skills for the Auraison platform. Scope includes:

1. Define a digital-twin schema and lifecycle model (create, update, sync, retire)
2. Implement asset-to-twin ingestion pipelines (sensor streams, CAD/URDF, point clouds)
3. Build a twin-state synchronisation layer (real-time and batch) between physical assets and their virtual counterparts
4. Integrate physics/simulation backends (e.g., Isaac Sim, Gazebo, MuJoCo) for predictive modelling
5. Expose twin query and actuation APIs through the control-plane
6. Provide visualisation hooks (Rerun, 3-D viewers) for twin state inspection
7. Add agent skills that let Claude Code agents create, query, and manipulate digital twins
8. End-to-end demo: twin of a reference robotic asset with live telemetry

A design doc in docs/plans/ is required before sub-issues are created (per CLAUDE.md planning policy).
title: Digital Twins — develop skills for constructing digital twins

close_reason: Design doc authored and merged to main
description:
The three-plane architecture (user / control / management) is missing a stabilisation layer: AgentOps — the operational runtime that governs agent behavioural dynamics between control-plane reasoning and management-plane governance.

## Position in the stack

  Management Plane (governance, cost, tenancy)
       ↑
  AgentOps Layer  ← NEW
       ↑
  Control Plane (planning, reasoning, task decomposition)
       ↓
  User Plane (execution mesh)

AgentOps = nervous system + reflexes. It stabilises behaviour before governance needs to intervene.

## Core responsibilities to design

- Execution mediation: concurrency limits, retries, timeout rules, scheduling order before claude -p subprocesses are spawned
- Agent lifecycle control: start/stop/pause/restart/isolate agents and workflows
- State stabilisation: checkpoints, state snapshots, resumability (extends current --resume session mechanism; must survive process restart)
- Runtime policy enforcement: max tool calls, recursion depth, token budgets, execution boundaries (distinct from management-plane infrastructure policy)
- Backpressure and throttling: prevent planner agents spawning unbounded Ray workers (GPU exhaustion / cascading failures)
- Observability at agent granularity: reasoning traces, tool call graphs, agent decision chains (feeds management-plane Observability Store)
- Guardrails engine: safety constraints, budget limits, behaviour policies (currently baked ad-hoc into each agent wrapper)

## Why this is needed now

Four failure modes at multi-agent scale:
1. Recursive planning loops — agent refines plan endlessly, no execution
2. Tool call storms — agents aggressively call kubectl/ray, API exhaustion
3. Distributed agent chaos — duplicate work, race conditions, conflicting cluster mutations
4. Retry explosions — naive retry logic without backoff or bound

In v1 these are mitigated by synchronous subprocess model (natural serialisation). In v1.5+ (async worker pool) they become critical.

## Mapping to auraison ecosystem

- Zenoh event mediation (user plane <> control plane)
- Ray actor scheduling limits (before NotebookAgent submits)
- Agent checkpointing (extends --resume)
- Notebook pipeline throttling
- Tool-call governance (precursor to management-plane Policy Engine)

## World Model-Driven AgentOps (frontier section)

Design should address how AgentOps can maintain a structured world model of:
- Active agent states and execution topology
- Environment state (cluster health, GPU utilisation, job queue depth)
- Causal graph linking control-plane intents to user-plane outcomes

Directly relevant to turtlebot-maze and VLA architectures: agent systems converging with world-model-based robotics.

## Interfaces with other planes

- Receives: AgentIntent from control plane (before subprocess spawn)
- Emits: AgentEvent to management-plane Observability Store
- Enforces: tool scope policies fetched from management-plane Policy Engine
- Controls: Ray worker pool size, kubectl rate limits (user plane)

## Evolution path

v1   — AgentOps implicit: synchronous subprocess = natural serialisation; no explicit layer
v1.5 — Introduce execution scheduler and retry bounds for async worker pool
v2   — Full AgentOps runtime: guardrails engine, checkpointing, world-model state, trace system

## Acceptance criteria

- Design doc at docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-auraison-agentops-design.md
- Mermaid component diagrams covering all core responsibilities
- AgentOps interfaces defined for all three existing planes
- World-model-driven AgentOps frontier documented with robotics relevance
- PR merged to main
title: Write AgentOps layer design doc

description:
Enrich docs/plans/2026-02-23-aiops-control-plane-design.md with a dedicated section on how the control plane communicates with the user plane.

The section must address the following:

## 1. The communication problem

The control plane (request/response, FastAPI) and the user plane (event-driven, real-time) have fundamentally different communication models. The design doc currently acknowledges this impedance mismatch but does not specify how it is bridged. This section fills that gap.

## 2. Communication planes and their protocols

Define which protocol is used for each direction and purpose:

| Direction | Protocol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Control plane → User plane (job dispatch) | Ray Jobs API / kubectl | Submit RayJob, receive ray_job_id |
| User plane → Control plane (lifecycle events) | HTTP webhook (POST /api/v1/jobs/{id}/copyback) | Job completion, notebook copyback |
| Control plane → User plane (cluster management) | kubectl / Helm via ClusterAgent | Scale, health check, CRD updates |
| User plane → Control plane (real-time telemetry) | TBD — Redis Streams or Kafka | Pod metrics, GPU utilisation, job logs |
| Claude Code → User plane (robot control) | ros-mcp-server over rosbridge WebSocket :9090 | Real-time Nav2 goals, ROS topic I/O |

## 3. v1 communication: synchronous subprocess + webhook

Document the v1 pattern in detail:
- NotebookAgent calls  (outbound, synchronous)
- User plane emits completion via copyback webhook (inbound, async HTTP)
- ClusterAgent uses kubectl controls the Kubernetes cluster manager.

 Find more information at: https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/

Basic Commands (Beginner):
  create          Create a resource from a file or from stdin
  expose          Take a replication controller, service, deployment or pod and expose it as a new Kubernetes service
  run             Run a particular image on the cluster
  set             Set specific features on objects

Basic Commands (Intermediate):
  explain         Get documentation for a resource
  get             Display one or many resources
  edit            Edit a resource on the server
  delete          Delete resources by file names, stdin, resources and names, or by resources and label selector

Deploy Commands:
  rollout         Manage the rollout of a resource
  scale           Set a new size for a deployment, replica set, or replication controller
  autoscale       Auto-scale a deployment, replica set, stateful set, or replication controller

Cluster Management Commands:
  certificate     Modify certificate resources.
  cluster-info    Display cluster information
  top             Display resource (CPU/memory) usage
  cordon          Mark node as unschedulable
  uncordon        Mark node as schedulable
  drain           Drain node in preparation for maintenance
  taint           Update the taints on one or more nodes

Troubleshooting and Debugging Commands:
  describe        Show details of a specific resource or group of resources
  logs            Print the logs for a container in a pod
  attach          Attach to a running container
  exec            Execute a command in a container
  port-forward    Forward one or more local ports to a pod
  proxy           Run a proxy to the Kubernetes API server
  cp              Copy files and directories to and from containers
  auth            Inspect authorization
  debug           Create debugging sessions for troubleshooting workloads and nodes

Advanced Commands:
  diff            Diff the live version against a would-be applied version
  apply           Apply a configuration to a resource by file name or stdin
  patch           Update fields of a resource
  replace         Replace a resource by file name or stdin
  wait            Experimental: Wait for a specific condition on one or many resources
  kustomize       Build a kustomization target from a directory or URL.

Settings Commands:
  label           Update the labels on a resource
  annotate        Update the annotations on a resource
  completion      Output shell completion code for the specified shell (bash, zsh, fish, or powershell)

Other Commands:
  alpha           Commands for features in alpha
  api-resources   Print the supported API resources on the server
  api-versions    Print the supported API versions on the server, in the form of "group/version"
  config          Modify kubeconfig files
  plugin          Provides utilities for interacting with plugins
  version         Print the client and server version information

Usage:
  kubectl [flags] [options]

Use "kubectl <command> --help" for more information about a given command.
Use "kubectl options" for a list of global command-line options (applies to all commands). for cluster state reads/writes (outbound, synchronous)
- The gap: no standardised inbound channel for real-time user-plane events (metrics, alerts)

## 4. v1.5 communication: Redis Streams as event bridge

Document how Redis Streams bridge the two planes without introducing a full broker:
- User plane worker pods publish job lifecycle events to Redis Stream (job.started, job.failed, job.completed)
- Control plane consumes the stream to update job state — replacing the pull-based polling model
- ClusterAgent publishes health events (node.gpu_oom, pod.crashloop) to a cluster-events stream
- The control plane UI subscribes via SSE or WebSocket for live dashboard updates

## 5. v2 communication: NATS + Kafka

Map the full streaming architecture from DRAFT-agentic-systems-architecture.md §3 onto the two planes:
- NATS for control messages (ms latency): job dispatch, cluster commands, agent task requests
- Kafka for durable streams (audit, replay): experiment data, notebook outputs, W&B metrics, GPU telemetry
- Retain rosbridge/ros-mcp-server for the ROS 2 interface — it is already the right protocol for that domain

## 6. Security boundary between planes

The control plane must not expose its internal state to user-plane workloads:
- User-plane pods communicate inbound only via a defined webhook surface (no direct DB access)
- Outbound control plane calls use scoped credentials (kubeconfig RBAC, Ray Jobs API token)
- ros-mcp-server interactions require explicit human approval for actuation commands (as implemented in turtlebot-maze /navigate)

## 7. Mermaid communication diagram

Add a sequence or flow diagram showing:
- The v1 communication paths (subprocess + webhook)
- The v1.5 Redis Streams bridge
- The v2 NATS/Kafka topology
Side-by-side or layered, showing evolution.
title: Update design doc: control plane ↔ user plane communication architecture

description:
Integrate NVIDIA Cosmos-Reason2 (https://github.com/nvidia-cosmos/cosmos-reason2) as a physical reasoning backbone for user-plane and control-plane agents.

## What is Cosmos-Reason2

A vision-language model (VLM) post-trained on physical common sense and embodied reasoning data via SFT + RL. Built on Qwen3-VL. Two variants:
- Cosmos-Reason2-2B (24 GB GPU min)
- Cosmos-Reason2-8B (32 GB GPU min)

Validated on H100, GB200, DGX Spark, Jetson AGX. Deployable via vLLM (≥0.11.0) or HuggingFace Transformers (≥4.57.0). Apache 2.0 (code) + NVIDIA Open Model License (weights).

## Capabilities relevant to Auraison

- Physical reasoning: space, time, physics comprehension
- Chain-of-thought / extended reasoning traces for embodied decisions
- Embodied planning: action selection and trajectory prediction
- Multi-modal input (image + video) for environmental understanding

## Proposed integration points

1. **User plane — ros.dev.gpu**: Deploy Cosmos-Reason2-8B as a vLLM inference server on the ros.dev.gpu RayCluster. User-plane agents (Nav2, behavior trees, YOLOv8) call it for spatial reasoning and action selection instead of relying solely on Claude Code.
2. **User plane — VLA backbone**: Replace or augment the VLA agent reasoning layer with Cosmos-Reason2 for physical common sense decisions (obstacle avoidance, goal reachability, terrain assessment).
3. **Control plane — CosmosAgent subprocess**: A new claude -p subprocess (CosmosAgent) that wraps the vLLM endpoint; used by the control plane to reason about robot state, twin state, and mission feasibility before dispatching jobs.
4. **Digital Twins (auraison-5z3)**: Feed twin state snapshots (pose, sensor readings) as context to Cosmos-Reason2 for physics-grounded reasoning about asset behaviour.

## Requirements before sub-issues

Per CLAUDE.md planning policy, a design doc (docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-cosmos-reason2-design.md) is required before epics and sub-issues are created. The design doc must cover:
- vLLM deployment on ros.dev.gpu / torch.dev.gpu RayCluster
- CosmosAgent tool scope and prompt interface
- Integration with TwinAgent (auraison-5z3)
- Model variant selection (2B vs 8B) and GPU budget
- Inference latency requirements for real-time vs batch use cases

## External reference

https://github.com/nvidia-cosmos/cosmos-reason2
title: Incorporate Cosmos-Reason2 reasoning agents for physical AI

description:
## Context

As Pydantic AI agents replace claude -p subprocesses across the planes (auraison-2a5), the platform needs structured observability of agent reasoning: chain-of-thought steps, tool calls, model responses, latency, and errors. Pydantic Logfire is the natural fit — it is natively integrated with Pydantic AI and provides OpenTelemetry-based tracing with first-class support for LLM spans.

## What Logfire captures for Pydantic AI agents

- Full reasoning traces: each agent.run() call produces a root span with child spans per reasoning step
- Tool call spans: name, arguments, result, duration
- Model request/response spans: prompt tokens, completion tokens, model name, finish reason
- Structured chain-of-thought: if the model produces thinking/reasoning content, it is captured as span attributes
- Exceptions and retries: validation errors, tool failures, model errors

## Integration points across planes

1. **Control plane agents** (NotebookAgent, ClusterAgent, WandBAgent, LakehouseAgent, TwinAgent, CosmosAgent): instrument each Pydantic AI agent with logfire.instrument_pydantic_ai(); all job dispatch and cluster management reasoning traces flow to Logfire
2. **Data plane agents** (LakehouseCatalog, ingestion, query agents): query planning and ingestion pipeline reasoning traces
3. **User plane agents** (VLA, behavior tree executor): physical reasoning traces from Cosmos-Reason2 backend — spatial reasoning steps, action selection rationale
4. **FastAPI layer**: logfire.instrument_fastapi() on the control-plane FastAPI app — request traces link to agent reasoning spans for end-to-end observability

## Key design decisions

- **Logfire project**: one project per plane (control, data, user) or one shared project with plane as a tag — TBD in design doc
- **Sensitive data scrubbing**: tool arguments and model outputs may contain credentials or PII; scrub config required
- **Sampling**: full traces for development; head-based sampling in production to control ingestion cost
- **Export**: Logfire cloud (default) vs self-hosted OTEL collector (v2 option for data sovereignty)
- **Correlation with W&B**: link Logfire trace IDs to W&B run IDs where applicable for experiment ↔ reasoning correlation

## Requirements before sub-issues

Per CLAUDE.md planning policy, a design doc (docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-logfire-observability-design.md) is required. Must cover:
- Logfire project structure and tag taxonomy
- Instrumentation pattern per agent type (Pydantic AI + FastAPI + asyncio)
- Sensitive data scrubbing strategy
- Sampling policy (dev vs production)
- Dashboard design: reasoning trace viewer, tool call heatmap, error rate per agent
- Correlation with W&B experiment tracking
title: Instrument Pydantic AI agents with Logfire for reasoning trace observability

Design doc: docs/plans/2026-02-22-coco-lakehouse-demo-design.md
Implementation plan: docs/plans/2026-02-22-coco-lakehouse-demo-plan.md

title: data-plane: implement lakehouse package — COCO-Caption Experiment #0

description:
## What is Cosmos-Transfer2.5

https://github.com/nvidia-cosmos/cosmos-transfer2.5

A multi-controlnet world foundation model that translates simulation video → photorealistic video (sim2real) and real video → augmented real video (real2real). Conditioned on multiple spatial control inputs (RGB, depth, segmentation, edge, blur). Built on top of Cosmos-Predict2.5.

Model variants:
- Cosmos-Transfer2.5-2B: general-purpose robotics and physical AI
- Cosmos-Transfer2.5-2B/robot-multiview-control: depth + edge + segmentation control for robotic tasks
- Distilled edge variants: optimised for low-latency deployment
Blackwell and ARM inference supported.

Documented result: +68.5% improvement in mission success rate on navigation tasks via hybrid sim + Transfer2.5 augmented training data (Cosmos Cookbook).

## Role in Auraison user plane

Cosmos-Transfer2.5 is the **sim2real bridge** in the user plane, sitting between Cosmos-Predict2 (world model) and real-world deployment:



This closes the sim2real gap for the turtlebot-maze reference application:
1. Gazebo generates synthetic rollouts cheaply at scale
2. Transfer2.5 makes them photorealistic
3. Augmented data fine-tunes VLA / Nav2 policies on torch.dev.gpu
4. Policies trained on augmented data transfer to physical robots without domain shift

## Proposed integration points

1. **Synthetic data generation pipeline on torch.dev.gpu**: Ray job that runs Gazebo rollouts → Cosmos-Predict2 → Cosmos-Transfer2.5 → Parquet dataset in lakehouse (data plane). Provides augmented training data for VLA and Nav2 policy fine-tuning.
2. **Pre-execution photorealistic rollout**: Before dispatching a Nav2 goal, generate a photorealistic predicted trajectory (Predict2 → Transfer2.5) for Cosmos-Reason2 to evaluate — more realistic visual input improves reasoning accuracy.
3. **Real2Real augmentation**: Augment existing turtlebot-maze ROS bag recordings with lighting/texture variation for more robust policy training.
4. **Robot-multiview-control variant**: Use depth + edge control maps from Gazebo to condition Transfer2.5 for geometrically accurate sim2real translation.

## Integration with existing epics

- **Cosmos-Predict2 (auraison-oys)**: Transfer2.5 is a post-processing stage on top of Predict2 output — Predict2 generates the future state video, Transfer2.5 makes it photorealistic. Design docs should be coordinated.
- **Cosmos-Reason2 (auraison-eh1)**: Transfer2.5 improves the visual quality of inputs to Reason2's pre-execution evaluation.
- **Digital Twins (auraison-5z3)**: Transfer2.5-augmented synthetic renders can populate twin state snapshots with visually realistic previews.
- **Data plane (auraison-ejd)**: Augmented video datasets stored in lakehouse as training artifacts.

## Requirements before sub-issues

Per CLAUDE.md planning policy, a design doc (docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-cosmos-transfer2-design.md) is required. Must cover:
- Deployment on torch.dev.gpu (Ray Serve vs standalone server; GPU memory budget alongside Predict2)
- Pipeline orchestration: Gazebo → Predict2 → Transfer2.5 → lakehouse
- Control map generation from Gazebo (depth, segmentation, edge extraction)
- Real2Real augmentation workflow for turtlebot-maze ROS bags
- Integration into pre-execution rollout loop (latency budget)
- User-plane design doc update: add Transfer2.5 to torch.dev.gpu environment table and SDG pipeline section
title: Incorporate Cosmos-Transfer2.5 sim2real augmentation into user plane

close_reason: Design doc updated with Mermaid diagrams, multi-agent rationale, first principles, event-driven analysis, agentic systems architecture alignment, three-plane model, and turtlebot-maze reference application.
description:
Enrich docs/plans/2026-02-23-aiops-control-plane-design.md with:

1. Mermaid diagrams replacing the ASCII art architecture block
   - System context diagram (C4 Level 1): UI → API → agents → KubeRay/W&B/lakehouse/eaia
   - Sequence diagram: notebook execution flow (eaia → POST /jobs → NotebookAgent → KubeRay → copyback)
   - Component diagram: agent layer showing tool scoping per agent

2. Multi-agent rationale section
   - Why one agent per domain (notebook, cluster, wandb, lakehouse) vs a single general agent
   - Tool scoping as a security and reliability boundary (--allowedTools per agent)
   - Session resume (--resume) enabling stateful multi-turn polling without re-invoking from scratch
   - Agent-to-API-route assignment table (which agent backs which router)

3. First-principles section: what drove this design
   - Subscription compute arbitrage: claude -p reuses Claude Code subscription, zero per-token marginal cost for internal tooling
   - Infrastructure-as-conversation: agents use the same CLIs a human operator would (kubectl, ray, wandb, dbt) — no new SDK surface to maintain
   - Blast-radius containment: tool scoping (Bash(kubectl *)) limits what each agent subprocess can do, equivalent to least-privilege service accounts
   - Separation of concerns via subprocess boundary: each agent is stateless from the API's perspective; failures are isolated; retries are clean
   - Avoid framework lock-in: claude -p subprocess is framework-agnostic — Pydantic AI can be layered in v2 without rewriting the agent logic
notes:
4. Event-driven vs request/response design analysis
   - Most AI agents in the wild are event-driven (event bus → agent reacts → emits events). This design uses synchronous request/response (API call → subprocess → return JSON). Why?
   - Compare event sourcing / CQRS pattern (immutable event log, agents as event consumers/producers) vs current approach (stateless subprocess per request, in-memory job store)
   - Trade-offs to address:
     * Event sourcing gives full audit trail and replayability — current approach loses history on restart (in-memory store)
     * Event-driven decouples producers from consumers — current tight coupling: API router directly invokes agent subprocess
     * Event sourcing naturally handles long-running async jobs (notebook execution can take minutes/hours) — current polling model is a workaround for the synchronous subprocess limitation
     * Redis (already in the architecture) is the natural bridge — could be used as event bus (pub/sub or streams) rather than just a task queue
   - Where the current design is justified:
     * Simplicity for v1 — no broker topology to operate
     * claude -p subprocess is inherently synchronous; async event consumption would require persistent agent processes or a worker pool
     * Session resume (--resume) partially compensates: long-running jobs split across multiple subprocess invocations with shared session context
   - Migration path: Redis Streams as event log → worker pool of claude -p agents subscribing to job events → natural event sourcing in v2 alongside Pydantic AI
5. Principles from DRAFT-agentic-systems-architecture.md and the control plane / user plane duality

Context: aiops is the CONTROL PLANE that operationally manages an agentic RUNTIME (user plane) where VLA agents, multi-agent systems, and real-time reasoning execute on KubeRay. This is a classic network architecture pattern (SDN control/data split) applied to agentic infrastructure. The design doc must articulate what this duality implies for the control plane's responsibilities.

Key principles from the draft to address:

A. Routing pattern (§2.3) and the current design
   - The current FastAPI router + specialist agents IS the routing pattern — each agent is a specialist with optimized prompts and tool scope for its domain
   - But the draft's routing pattern is event-driven (publish to categorical topics, agents subscribe)
   - The design doc should acknowledge this: the current synchronous routing is a v1 simplification; v2 should move to NATS or Kafka topics per agent domain

B. Orchestrator-Worker (§2.5) and control plane identity
   - aiops IS the orchestrator: it breaks down user intent (submit notebook, check cluster, query W&B) into agent subtasks
   - KubeRay workers are the downstream executors
   - The draft's NATS-based orchestration (Orchestrator publishes to task/vision/*, workers subscribe and reply) is the target pattern for the control plane's agent dispatch layer
   - Currently: synchronous subprocess. Target: NATS request-reply for sub-second control messages

C. Streaming technology selection (§3.1)
   - NATS: low-latency control messages → right choice for control plane ↔ KubeRay agent dispatch
   - Kafka: durable event log with replay → right choice for experiment data, notebook outputs, W&B metrics
   - Redis (already in architecture): bridge technology for v1.5 — Redis Streams can approximate Kafka semantics before full Kafka adoption
   - The design doc should include a streaming technology decision matrix specific to this system

D. Memory management (§4) and stateless subprocess limitation
   - Current agents have only short-term memory (claude --resume session context)
   - The user plane agents (VLA, multi-agent) will generate episodic memory: past job outcomes, cluster failure patterns, experiment regressions
   - The control plane should expose a memory API: job history, cluster health timeline, W&B run lineage
   - Long-term: the control plane becomes the episodic + semantic memory store for the agentic runtime it manages

E. Observability and the audit trail (§6.4)
   - The draft's Kafka audit trail schema (agent_id, timestamp, reasoning_trace, action_taken, outcome) should inform the control plane's job record schema
   - Currently Job model stores minimal fields; it should grow to capture agent reasoning traces, tool calls made, and outcomes — enabling replay and post-hoc analysis
   - This is also the bridge to event sourcing: job events as an immutable append-only log

F. Human oversight (§6.3) and VLA safety
   - The user plane runs VLA agents in robotics and industrial automation contexts (torch.dev.gpu, ros.dev.gpu environments)
   - The control plane must implement safety checkpoints: max_iterations per job, confidence thresholds before irreversible actions, human-in-the-loop escalation
   - These are not just operational concerns — they are safety requirements for the agentic runtime

G. MCP integration trajectory (§6.1)
   - The draft shows Pydantic AI + MCP as the structured integration pattern
   - Current: claude -p subprocess (MCP implicit via Claude Code)
   - v2: Pydantic AI agents with explicit MCP tool schemas — the control plane becomes an MCP server that the user plane agents can query for job status, cluster state, and experiment results

Summary for the design doc: the aiops control plane is not just job scheduling infrastructure — it is the memory, observability, safety, and orchestration substrate for a real-time agentic runtime. The architectural evolution from v1 (synchronous subprocess) to v2 (event-driven, streaming, Pydantic AI + MCP) must be planned with the user plane's real-time and safety requirements as first-class constraints.
6. Three-plane architecture and the turtlebot-maze reference application

## The three-plane model

The design doc must articulate that aiops implements a three-plane architecture — a pattern from telecom and SDN that maps cleanly to agentic infrastructure:

  USER PLANE      — where customer agents reason and act in real-time
  CONTROL PLANE   — this repo; creates, deploys, and oversees user plane instances
  MANAGEMENT PLANE — billing, tenancy, quotas, user management (non-goal v1, must be designed for)

This is not incidental naming. The planes have fundamentally different latency, consistency, and availability requirements:
- User plane: real-time (ms), stateful per-session, failure = robot stops or experiment diverges
- Control plane: seconds, eventually consistent job store, failure = degraded visibility but user plane continues
- Management plane: minutes, strongly consistent (billing must not lose events), independent lifecycle

## The turtlebot-maze reference application (user plane exemplar)

Repository: /mnt/nvme_internal_drive/robotics/ground/navigation/turtlebot-maze
Environments: ros.dev.gpu (KubeRay) — one of the two GPU environments this control plane manages

The turtlebot-maze demonstrates exactly what the user plane looks like in practice:
- Gazebo simulation + ROS 2 Jazzy + Nav2 running on KubeRay ros.dev.gpu workers
- Behavior trees (py_trees / BehaviorTree.CPP) for autonomous navigation and object search
- YOLOv8 object detection via PyTorch (Zenoh transport, zero ROS dependencies in detector container)
- Visual SLAM (stella_vslam) for mapping and localization
- Claude Code + /navigate slash command + ros-mcp-server (MCP over rosbridge WebSocket at :9090)
  → User types /navigate → Claude Code uses ros-mcp-server tools → rosbridge → Nav2 action server
  → This is a Claude Code agent doing real-time robot control: the user plane in operation

The user plane communication stack in this application:
  Gazebo (DDS/Cyclone) → Zenoh bridge → detector/SLAM containers (no ROS deps)
  rosbridge WebSocket (:9090) → ros-mcp-server → Claude Code MCP tools → /navigate skill

## What the control plane must provide for this application

The turtlebot-maze runs on ros.dev.gpu. The control plane is responsible for:
1. Provisioning: deploying the ros.dev.gpu RayCluster (infra epic, l5h.3)
2. Job dispatch: submitting notebook execution jobs (papermill runs that set up or evaluate the robot scenario)
3. Experiment tracking: W&B runs linked to navigation trials (success rate, path efficiency, detection accuracy)
4. Copyback: returning executed notebooks to eaia for MDX regeneration (documentation of results)
5. Observability: surfacing cluster health, GPU utilization, job status to the UI

Critically: the control plane does NOT control the robot in real-time. That is the user plane's job (ros-mcp-server + Nav2). The control plane manages the infrastructure the user plane runs on and the experiment lifecycle around it.

## Design requirement: the demo must close the loop end-to-end

The design doc must include a worked example showing all three planes in operation using turtlebot-maze:

  User → /navigate (Claude Code, user plane, real-time)
         ↕  rosbridge/ros-mcp-server
         ROS 2 Nav2 on ros.dev.gpu RayCluster (user plane compute)

  eaia → POST /api/v1/jobs (control plane)
         NotebookAgent → KubeRay ros.dev.gpu job (papermill run: navigation scenario setup)
         W&B run linked to job (experiment tracking)
         Copyback webhook → eaia MDX regeneration

  Management plane (v2): GPU hours consumed, per-user quota, billing event emitted

This end-to-end demo proves the architecture, validates the ros.dev.gpu environment, and provides a concrete target for the infra epic (l5h.2 and l5h.3 specifically).

## Implications for the design doc sections

- Mermaid diagrams (section 1): add a third diagram showing the three planes with turtlebot-maze as the user plane exemplar
- First principles (section 3): the plane separation is itself a first principle — it enforces that user plane failures do not cascade to the control plane, and control plane outages do not halt running agents
- Event-driven analysis (section 4): the user plane IS event-driven (DDS topics, Zenoh pub/sub, ROS actions); the control plane wraps this in a request/response job API — the design should acknowledge this impedance mismatch explicitly
- Reference: turtlebot-maze at /mnt/nvme_internal_drive/robotics/ground/navigation/turtlebot-maze
title: Update design doc: Mermaid diagrams, multi-agent rationale, and first-principles derivation

description:
## What is Cosmos-Predict2

https://github.com/nvidia-cosmos/cosmos-predict2

NVIDIA's foundation world model for physical AI. Specialises in future state prediction through visual simulation:
- **Video-to-World**: generate future video frames from an initial image/video + text prompt — predicts what will happen next
- **Text-to-World**: generate simulation sequences from text descriptions
- **Text-to-Image**: environment synthesis from natural language
- **Action-conditioned generation**: post-trained on robot datasets (GR00T, Bridge, AgiBotWorld) to predict outcomes of specific robot actions

Model variants: 0.6B, 2B, 14B. Specialised robotics variants (GR00T Dreams, DROID, Bridge). Requires Ampere GPU or newer, CUDA 12.6. Hugging Face diffusers (≥0.34.0).

## Role in Auraison

Cosmos-Predict2 is the **predictive simulation layer** of the user plane, complementing:
- **Cosmos-Reason2 (auraison-eh1)**: Reason2 reasons about what action to take; Predict2 simulates what will happen if that action is taken — closing the plan → predict → execute loop
- **Digital Twins (auraison-5z3)**: Predict2 generates predicted future twin states from current twin state + proposed action; the twin accumulates both observed and predicted state snapshots
- **Gazebo (current)**: Predict2 operates at the video/visual level; Gazebo provides physics-based simulation; together they give both physics-accurate and visually-realistic prediction

## Proposed integration in user plane

1. **Inference server on torch.dev.gpu**: Deploy Cosmos-Predict2 (2B or 14B variant) as a diffusers-backed inference server on the torch.dev.gpu RayCluster (GPU budget TBD in design doc)
2. **Pre-execution trajectory simulation**: Before a Nav2 goal is dispatched, the user-plane agent calls Predict2 with current camera frame + planned action → generates predicted video of the trajectory → Reason2 evaluates feasibility → go/no-go decision
3. **Post-training on turtlebot-maze dataset**: Fine-tune Predict2 on turtlebot-maze ROS bag recordings (video + action pairs) to get a domain-specific predictive model for the reference environment
4. **Twin state forecasting**: TwinAgent calls Predict2 to generate predicted future state snapshots for a twin given a proposed action sequence — stored in twins/state_snapshots with source='prediction'
5. **GR00T Dreams integration**: Use the pre-trained GR00T Dreams robotics variant as starting point before domain-specific fine-tuning

## Design spec updates required

Per the user's note, this should be incorporated into relevant existing design specs:
- **docs/plans/2026-02-23-auraison-user-plane-design.md**: Add Cosmos-Predict2 as a named user-plane component; extend the torch.dev.gpu environment table; add predict → reason → execute loop to the reference application section
- **docs/plans/2026-03-02-digital-twins-design.md (auraison-5z3)**: Add predicted state snapshots to the schema (source='prediction'); document TwinAgent → Predict2 call pattern
- **New design doc**: docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-cosmos-predict2-design.md — full integration design per CLAUDE.md planning policy

## Requirements before sub-issues

Per CLAUDE.md planning policy, a design doc is required covering:
- Model variant selection (0.6B vs 2B vs 14B) and GPU budget on torch.dev.gpu
- Inference server deployment pattern (Ray Serve vs standalone vLLM/diffusers server)
- Pre-execution simulation loop: latency budget, integration with Nav2 action server
- Post-training pipeline: turtlebot-maze ROS bag → video+action dataset → fine-tuning job on torch.dev.gpu
- Twin state forecasting schema extension
- Interaction with Cosmos-Reason2 (auraison-eh1): how Predict2 output feeds Reason2 evaluation
title: Incorporate Cosmos-Predict2 world model for predictive simulation in user plane

close_reason: Design doc authored and merged to main
description:
The three-plane model governs control flow (execution / decision / governance) but does not govern data flow. As agentic workloads grow, the lakehouse becomes system memory — a persistent world model — not merely analytics infrastructure. This requires a dedicated data plane.

## The core insight

In traditional software: logic → data
In agentic systems:    data → reasoning → action

Data becomes the substrate of cognition. The lakehouse is not a single-plane component — it spans planes — and needs its own architectural treatment.

## Definition

The data plane governs: movement, storage, transformation, and accessibility of data across the entire system. It is orthogonal to reasoning or execution.

## Position in the architecture

The data plane sits horizontally — all planes interact with it:

  Management Plane  →  governs storage (policies, lifecycle, access control)
                              ↕
  AgentOps Layer    →  checkpointing, world-model snapshots
                              ↕
  Control Plane     →  reads semantic memory, historical context, world state
                              ↕
  Data Plane ←——— centerpiece: lakehouse + embeddings + event logs + agent traces
                              ↕
  User Plane        →  writes operational data (telemetry, perception, job outputs)

## Migration: aegean-ai/lakehouse → data-plane/

The existing aegean-ai/lakehouse repo contains design specs and code for the lakehouse
(dbt models, ingestion pipelines, schema definitions). This work must be consolidated into
the auraison monorepo under data-plane/:

1. Audit aegean-ai/lakehouse: inventory all design docs, dbt models, pipelines, and config
2. Move design specs to docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-auraison-data-plane-design.md (or docs/architecture/ if system-level)
3. Move code (dbt project, ingestion scripts, schema files) to data-plane/ in this monorepo
4. Update LakehouseAgent (control-plane/backend/agents/lakehouse_agent.py) to reference the new data-plane/ path via REPO_ROOT
5. Archive or redirect aegean-ai/lakehouse with a pointer to this monorepo
6. Update CLAUDE.md to reflect data-plane/ in the repository layout

This follows the same pattern used when consolidating user-plane and management-plane into the monorepo.

## Core responsibilities to design

### A. Persistent storage
- Lakehouse (Iceberg / Delta / Parquet) — migrated from aegean-ai/lakehouse
- Object store (S3-compatible via Cloudflare R2 or local MinIO)
- Feature store (structured inputs for VLA and ML models)
- Embeddings store (semantic retrieval for agents)

### B. Data lifecycle
- Ingestion pipelines (user plane → lakehouse)
- Schema versioning and evolution
- Partitioning strategy (by tenant, environment, date)
- Retention and archival policies

### C. Data serving
- Semantic retrieval for control-plane agents (RAG over agent traces, job history)
- Batch training data for VLA model fine-tuning
- Streaming access for real-time telemetry consumers

### D. Data consistency
- Schema lineage (dbt models, already in aegean-ai/lakehouse)
- Reproducibility: any job result must be reconstructible from the data plane
- World-model snapshots: point-in-time environment state for agent replay

## The lakehouse as persistent world model

In agentic robotics systems, the lakehouse accumulates:
- Perception outputs (GeoParquet, point clouds, detection events)
- Zenoh event history (ROS topic recordings)
- Agent reasoning traces (AgentEvent records from AgentOps)
- World model snapshots (environment state at decision points)
- Experiment results (W&B runs linked to jobs)

This is not just storage — it is the persistent memory substrate that enables:
- Agents to reason over historical context without re-executing
- VLA model fine-tuning from accumulated robot experience
- Post-hoc causal analysis of agent decisions

## Critical distinction: data plane vs management plane

Management plane governs the data plane (policies, access control, lifecycle) but does not own it.

| Data Plane             | Management Plane      |
|------------------------|----------------------|
| stores data            | governs storage       |
| serves queries         | defines policy        |
| enables reasoning      | enforces compliance   |
| manages schemas        | manages access rights |

## Mapping to auraison ecosystem

### User plane → writes
- Segmentation outputs → Parquet tables
- Nav2 path traces → append logs
- YOLOv8 detection events → structured tables
- Gazebo simulation telemetry → Zenoh event history

### Control plane → reads
- Job history for NotebookAgent context
- Cluster failure patterns for ClusterAgent
- Prior experiment results for WandBAgent
- dbt model dependency graph for LakehouseAgent

### LakehouseAgent (already exists)
The LakehouseAgent (Bash(dbt *), Read, Edit) is the control plane's interface to the data plane. Design should formalise this as the data-plane API boundary. After migration, it points to data-plane/ via REPO_ROOT.

## Interfaces with other planes

- Ingestion API: accepts write events from user plane workers (Ray job outputs)
- Query API: serves semantic retrieval requests from control-plane agents
- Snapshot API: accepts world-model snapshots from AgentOps checkpointing
- Policy interface: accepts retention/access rules from management plane

## Evolution path

v1   — Data plane implicit: lakehouse operated manually via LakehouseAgent; no formal ingestion
v1.5 — Formalise ingestion pipeline: user plane workers write structured events to lakehouse
v2   — Semantic retrieval layer: embeddings + RAG for control-plane agent memory
       World-model snapshots: AgentOps writes point-in-time state; enables agent replay
v3   — VLA training pipeline: accumulated perception data → fine-tuning loop

## Acceptance criteria

- aegean-ai/lakehouse audited and its content inventoried
- Design specs moved to docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-auraison-data-plane-design.md

description:
Ingest the full VisDrone benchmark (https://github.com/VisDrone/VisDrone-Dataset) into the MinIO/DuckLake lakehouse for all five tasks:

- Task 1: Object Detection in Images (VisDrone-DET)
- Task 2: Object Detection in Video (VisDrone-VID)
- Task 3: Single-Object Tracking (VisDrone-SOT)
- Task 4: Multi-Object Tracking (VisDrone-MOT)
- Task 5: Crowd Counting (VisDrone-CC)

Acceptance criteria:
- Use sync_from_hf (or equivalent download pipeline) to pull all task splits (train/val/test) from the VisDrone GitHub releases or a HF mirror
- Convert annotations to Parquet and store under a consistent lakehouse schema (dataset=visdrone, task=<task-id>, split=<split>)
- Register dataset catalog entries in DuckDB/DuckLake via LakehouseCatalog
- Confirm all splits queryable via LakehouseQuery
- Add integration test covering at minimum Task 1 (DET) round-trip ingest → query
title: data-plane: ingest VisDrone dataset (all tasks) into lakehouse
